statement.p: Scientists are conducting an experiment with a gas in a sealed container. The mass of the gas is measured, and the scientists realize that the gas is leaking over time in a linear way.
Ten minutes since the experiment started, the gas had a mass of 217 grams.
Eighteen minutes since the experiment started, the gas had a mass of 167.4 grams.

Let x be the number of minutes that have passed since the experiment started, and let y be the mass of the gas in grams at that moment. Use a linear equation to model the weight of the gas over time.
This lines slope-intercept equation is .
40 minutes after the experiment started, there would be grams of gas left.
If a linear model continues to be accurate, minutes since the experiment started, all gas in the container will be gone.

li.p: A lines equation in slope-intercept form looks like y=mx+b, where m is the slope, and y is the y-coordinate of the y-intercept. We first need to find the lines slope. To find the slope, we need two points on the line. We have been given that (10,217) and (18,167.4) are points on the linear model, so we can use the slope formula:
First, we mark which number corresponds to which variable in the formula:
Now we substitute these numbers into the corresponding variables in the slope formula:
This lines slope is -6.2. This implies that the gas is leaking with a rate of 6.2 grams per minute.
So now we have that y=-6.2x+b. The next step is to find the value of b. There are at least two ways.
One way is to substitute one of the given points into y=-6.2x+b. We choose to use (10,217).

p.m: \begin{aligned} y \amp = -6.2x + b \\ 217 \amp = -6.2 \cdot 10 + b \\ 217 \amp = -62 + b \\ 217\mathbf{{} + 62} \amp = -62 + b\mathbf{{} + 62} \\ 279 \amp = b\\ b \amp = 279 \end{aligned}

li.p: This lines slope-intercept equation is y=-6.2x+279.
Another way is to use the lines point-slope equation y-y_{1}=m(x-x_{1}). Again, we choose to use the point (10,217).
This is the second way to find b, and thus the lines slope-intercept equation is y=-6.2x+279.
After 40 minutes, we can find the mass of the remaining gas if we substitute 40 in for x in the equation y=-6.2x+279. We have:
So after 40 minutes, there are 31 grams of gas remaining.
To find when the gas will be all gone, we need to find when there are 0 grams of gas left. We can substitute 0 in for y in the equation y=-6.2x+279, and we have:
So after 45 minutes, the gas will be all gone.
